topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The smell of aged paper and stale coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found the manuscript tucked beneath a stack of utility bills, a thick, cream-colored volume bound in worn leather. My jaw tightened as I lifted it, the weight of it feeling heavy and offensive in my hands. It was *my* life, meticulously transcribed, dissected, and polished into some grand, fictional narrative. I flipped through the pages, my eyes scanning the familiar contours of my routine, the mundane arguments, the fleeting moments of genuine laughter—all rendered with an unnerving, almost clinical precision. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a bitter taste in my mouth. When he walked in, humming tunelessly, I didn't look up immediately. I just held the book, letting the silence stretch, letting the sheer audacity of the endeavor settle between us like dust. "That's... quite something," I managed, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, the glow of the laptop screen illuminating the focused intensity in his eyes. It was late, and the only sound was the rhythmic tap-tap-tap of his fingers on the keyboard. Then I saw it—the title page, stark and dramatic, featuring my face, though subtly altered, draped in shadows and dramatic lighting. A sharp, unpleasant clenching started in my chest, a tight knot pulling at my ribs. I walked over, not to congratulate him, but to snatch the document. My fingers brushed against the glossy paper, and I recoiled as if burned. "What is this?" I asked, my tone sharp, laced with an edge that made him pause mid-sentence. When he finally looked up, a flicker of surprise crossed his face, quickly replaced by something guarded. I didn't offer an explanation, just a deliberate straightening of my posture, a subtle withdrawal that spoke volumes about my current assessment of the situation.

[story 3]

I found the evidence during a routine tidying session. Tucked inside a rarely used drawer, beneath old tax documents, lay the manuscript. The sheer effort involved—the dedication, the sheer imaginative labor—felt like an insult to the reality of our shared existence. I picked it up, the texture of the paper rough beneath my fingertips, and felt a distinct tightening around my eyes. I didn't need to read the content to understand the implication; the meticulous charting of my vulnerabilities, the way he had framed our disagreements as high drama, it was suffocating. I placed the book back down with exaggerated care, ensuring the sound of the closing drawer echoed in the quiet room. "You really went to all that trouble," I said, my voice dangerously level, the inflection suggesting that the effort expended was entirely misplaced and frankly, tiresome.
